title: Hello from the Phone
lesson:
On iOS (and macOS, which shares its Darwin core), system services are reached through 'svc #0' with the call number in x16. We're using a small set: x16 = 1 prints an integer in x0, x16 = 4 prints a zero-terminated string at the address in x0, and x16 = 93 exits.

.asciz lays a string into memory followed by a zero byte. 'mov x0, #0x1000' points x0 at the start of .data (where our string lives). Set x16 to 4, svc #0, and the kernel-stub prints the string. Same pattern as the LC-3's PUTS and MIPS's syscall 4, in different syntax. Pattern recognition is the whole point.
prompt: Print HELLO using svc #0. Put the string in .asciz at the start of .data, point x0 at it, set x16 = 4, svc, then exit.
starterCode:
.data
msg:    .asciz "HELLO"
.text
        mov x0, #0x1000      // address of msg
        mov x16, #4          // svc 4 = print zero-terminated string at x0
        svc #0
        mov x16, #93
        svc #0
hints: mov x0, #0x1000 puts the address of msg into x0.
mov x16, #4 then svc #0 prints the string until it hits the zero byte.
Finish with mov x16, #93 + svc #0 to exit.
reference:
.data
msg: .asciz "HELLO"
.text
mov x0, #0x1000
mov x16, #4
svc #0
mov x16, #93
svc #0
